_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Lafayette County, WI. Records are good from county formation (1846, from Iowa County). The lead mining era generated distinctive records — mining claims, territorial land office entries, and Cornish immigrant documentation.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Darlington in 1861.
Replaced by current building. Records transferred.

record_losses.description: Lafayette County records survive from county formation. Early mining-era records are in the territorial-era Iowa County records (before Lafayette was separated). Some earliest records may require checking Iowa County files at the Wisconsin Historical Society.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in the lead mining district before 1846, check Iowa County records — Lafayette was carved from Iowa County. Post-1846 records are at the Lafayette County courthouse in Darlington.
record_losses.source: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records.

deeds.completeness: Good — from county formation. Pre-1846 deeds in Iowa County records.
deeds.gaps: No major gaps post-formation. Mining claims pre-1846 in territorial records.
deeds.repository: Lafayette County Register of Deeds; Wisconsin Historical Society

wills_estates.repository: Lafayette County Probate Court; Wisconsin Historical Society

marriages.repository: Lafayette County Register of Deeds; Wisconsin Historical Society

court_minutes.repository: Lafayette County Clerk of Courts; Wisconsin Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Statewide registration from 1907. Pre-1907 rely on church records and census.

substitute_sources.source_type: Territorial land office records / mining claims
substitute_sources.description: Lead mining claims from the 1820s-1840s were filed with the territorial land office. These records document miners by name, claim location, and date — often the earliest documentation of an individual in the lead district.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (General Land Office records); Wisconsin Historical Society; Bureau of Land Management GLO records online
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing lead miners. Mining claims predate most other record types and can establish an individual's presence in the district before county government existed.
substitute_sources.source: Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932.

substitute_sources.description: Methodist and Congregational churches served the Cornish and Welsh mining communities. Records include baptisms, marriages, and burials.
substitute_sources.repository: Wisconsin Historical Society; individual congregations; Methodist archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Cornish families were strongly Methodist. Church records supplement county records, particularly before statewide vital registration.
substitute_sources.source: Copeland, A. F. 'The Cornish in Southwest Wisconsin.' Collections of the State Historical Society of Wisconsin 14 (1898).

narrative_hooks: Lafayette County was carved from Iowa County in 1846 — if {ancestor_name} mined lead here before that date, the earliest records are in Iowa County files at the Wisconsin Historical Society.
Cornish miners left records in the land office before the courthouse existed — {ancestor_name}'s mining claim may be the oldest document connecting the family to Wisconsin.
The county seat moved from Shullsburg to Darlington in 1861, and the records moved with it — check both locations for the earliest documentation of {ancestor_name}.
Wisconsin vital registration began in 1907 — Cornish Methodist church records fill the gap for {ancestor_name}'s family before that date.
source_references: Wisconsin Historical Society. Guide to Wisconsin County Records.
Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932.
Copeland, A. F. 'The Cornish in Southwest Wisconsin.' Collections of the State Historical Society of Wisconsin 14 (1898).
